lines: Let's begin by settling in. You don't need to fix anything right now. You don't need to solve anything. For these next few minutes, the only task is to be here.
Start with your breath. We'll use a pattern called box breathing — it's simple, and it gives your nervous system something steady to hold onto.
Breathe in through your nose for four.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for four.
Good. Let the box pattern soften. Breathe naturally now, but keep that gentle steadiness. Your body is already slowing down. Trust it.

I want to start with your body, because anxiety doesn't just live in your mind. It takes up residence in your flesh. It tightens things. It braces things. And sometimes, before you can think your way through worry, you need to feel your way into it. You need to know where it lives.
So let's do that. A slow scan. No rushing.

Bring your attention to your feet. Just notice them. The soles pressing against the floor, or tucked beneath you — whatever position they're in. Feel the contact. The weight. The temperature. There's nothing to fix here. Just arrive in your feet.
Now move up to your legs. Your shins. Your knees. Your thighs. Are they tight? Restless? Heavy? Numb? There's no wrong answer. Just notice what's actually here — not what you think should be here.

Now your stomach. This is where many people hold anxiety — a knot, a churning, a hollowness, a fluttering. Some of you felt it the moment I said the word. Don't try to relax it. Just place your attention there, the way you'd place a hand on a friend's shoulder. Gently. Just... I see you. I know you're there.

Breathe into your stomach now. Imagine your breath is warm, and you're directing it there. Not to fix the feeling. Just to accompany it. Inhale toward the tension. Exhale, and let whatever wants to soften... soften. Whatever doesn't... that's fine too.

Your chest. This is the other home of anxiety — tightness, compression, like someone's sitting on you. Or a fluttering behind the breastbone. Or a heaviness. What do you find? Just notice. Breathe around it.
Your throat. Anxiety often grips here — a lump, a constriction, a sense of words trapped or breath narrowed. Pay attention. What's the quality of the tightness? Does it have a shape? A temperature?
Your jaw. Are your teeth clenched? Even slightly? Let your mouth come open just a fraction. Teeth apart. Tongue resting against the roof of your mouth, loose.
Your forehead. Your temples. The space behind your eyes. Is there a furrow? A pressing? A band of tension? Notice it. Breathe.
Your shoulders. Are they crept up toward your ears? Let them drop. Not forcefully. Just... stop holding them up for a moment.

Now take one slow breath — a big one — and as you exhale, let everything soften at once. Not forcing relaxation. Not demanding peace. Just... inviting your body to let go of whatever it's willing to let go of right now.
Good. Whatever you found in that scan — tension, tightness, nothing, everything — that's honest information. That's your body telling you the truth about how you're carrying this day.
Before we go further, I want to say something that might be important for you to hear, and I want you to receive it slowly.

Anxiety is not a failure of faith. Worry is not disobedience. The saints worried. The psalmists panicked. David cried out from caves. Elijah sat under a tree and asked God to let him die. Jesus himself — Jesus, the Son of God — sweated blood in a garden the night before his death. His body shook with dread. He asked for the cup to be taken away.

If you've ever felt ashamed of your anxiety — as though a real Christian wouldn't feel this way, as though stronger faith would make the worry stop — let that go. Right now. Let it go. God is not disappointed in you for being afraid. He made your nervous system. He knows how it works. He knows the cost of being human, because he paid it himself.

God, I bring you this worry. Not because I've figured out how to stop worrying — I haven't. Not because I'm strong enough to just hand it over — some days I'm not. But because you told me I could come to you with it. You said come, and I'm coming.

I don't ask you to take the anxiety away. I've asked that before and sometimes it stays. So instead I ask: be in it with me. Steady my breath when my chest is tight. Loosen my jaw when I'm clenching against the future. Remind me, in the moments I forget, that I am not carrying this alone.

I know the birds don't worry, and I know I'm worth more than they are. But I'm not a bird, Lord. I'm a person with a mind that races and a body that braces and a heart that sometimes forgets how to trust. Meet me there. In the racing. In the bracing. In the forgetting.

Teach me to hold my worries loosely. To stop gripping the future so hard that I miss the ground beneath my feet right now. And when the worry comes back — because it will, we both know it will — help me remember this moment. This breath. Your presence. This invitation to be still and know.
Not because I'm strong enough. Because you are.
Take one more slow breath. Feel your body in this chair, on this floor, in this room. Feel the air on your skin. Hear the sounds around you. The world is still here. You are still here.
You are here. You are not alone. And this moment — this one breath, right now — is enough.

When you're ready, gently open your eyes. Move slowly. There's no rush. Carry whatever stillness you found with you — not as a demand on yourself, but as a gift. Even if it's small. Even if it's fragile. It's yours. And you can come back to it.
